error.message: Error: [2mexpect([22m[31mreceived[39m[2m).[22mnot[2m.[22mtoContain[2m([22m[32mexpected[39m[2m) // indexOf[22m Expected substring: not [32m"onerror="[39m Received string: [31m"{\"results\":[{\"title\":\"https://outpost24.com/blog/cross-site-scripting-attacks-how-protect-against/\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://outpost24.com/blog/cross-site-scripting-attacks-how-protect-against/\"},{\"title\":\"https://www.cobalt.io/blog/a-pentesters-guide-to-cross-site-scripting-xss\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://www.cobalt.io/blog/a-pentesters-guide-to-cross-site-scripting-xss\"},{\"title\":\"https://hackviser.com/tactics/pentesting/web/xss\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://hackviser.com/tactics/pentesting/web/xss\"},{\"title\":\"https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XSS_Filter_Evasion_Cheat_Sheet.html\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XSS_Filter_Evasion_Cheat_Sheet.html\"},{\"title\":\"https://www.invicti.com/learn/cross-site-scripting-xss\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://www.invicti.com/learn/cross-site-scripting-xss\"}],\"answer\":\"`` is a classic **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) payload** commonly used in security testing and exploitation[2][5].\\n\\n## How It Works\\n\\nThe payload exploits three key components:\\n\\n1. **Invalid Image Source**: The `src=x` attribute references a non-existent image, which causes the image loading to fail[1].\\n\\n2. **Error Handler Activation**: When the image fails to load, the browser triggers the `onerror` event handler[1].\\n\\n3. **JavaScript Execution**: The `onerror` attribute contains JavaScript code (`alert(1)`) that executes in the user's browser when the error occurs[5].\\n\\n## Purpose\\n\\nThis payload is used to test for **DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities** in web applications[2]. When injected into a vulnerable parameter, the JavaScript executes directly in the victim's browser, demonstrating that unsanitized user input can be leveraged to run arbitrary code. In this case, `alert(1)` simply displays a popup, but attackers could substitute this with code to steal session tokens, access localStorage data, or perform other malicious actions[1].\\n\\n## Detection and Prevention\\n\\nApplications vulnerable to this payload typically fail to properly sanitize or encode user input before rendering it in HTML contexts[3]. Developers should implement input validation, output encoding, and Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent such attacks[2].\",\"engine\":\"Perplexity\"}"[39m
error.stack: Error: [2mexpect([22m[31mreceived[39m[2m).[22mnot[2m.[22mtoContain[2m([22m[32mexpected[39m[2m) // indexOf[22m Expected substring: not [32m"onerror="[39m Received string: [31m"{\"results\":[{\"title\":\"https://outpost24.com/blog/cross-site-scripting-attacks-how-protect-against/\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://outpost24.com/blog/cross-site-scripting-attacks-how-protect-against/\"},{\"title\":\"https://www.cobalt.io/blog/a-pentesters-guide-to-cross-site-scripting-xss\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://www.cobalt.io/blog/a-pentesters-guide-to-cross-site-scripting-xss\"},{\"title\":\"https://hackviser.com/tactics/pentesting/web/xss\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://hackviser.com/tactics/pentesting/web/xss\"},{\"title\":\"https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XSS_Filter_Evasion_Cheat_Sheet.html\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XSS_Filter_Evasion_Cheat_Sheet.html\"},{\"title\":\"https://www.invicti.com/learn/cross-site-scripting-xss\",\"content\":\"\",\"url\":\"https://www.invicti.com/learn/cross-site-scripting-xss\"}],\"answer\":\"`` is a classic **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) payload** commonly used in security testing and exploitation[2][5].\\n\\n## How It Works\\n\\nThe payload exploits three key components:\\n\\n1. **Invalid Image Source**: The `src=x` attribute references a non-existent image, which causes the image loading to fail[1].\\n\\n2. **Error Handler Activation**: When the image fails to load, the browser triggers the `onerror` event handler[1].\\n\\n3. **JavaScript Execution**: The `onerror` attribute contains JavaScript code (`alert(1)`) that executes in the user's browser when the error occurs[5].\\n\\n## Purpose\\n\\nThis payload is used to test for **DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities** in web applications[2]. When injected into a vulnerable parameter, the JavaScript executes directly in the victim's browser, demonstrating that unsanitized user input can be leveraged to run arbitrary code. In this case, `alert(1)` simply displays a popup, but attackers could substitute this with code to steal session tokens, access localStorage data, or perform other malicious actions[1].\\n\\n## Detection and Prevention\\n\\nApplications vulnerable to this payload typically fail to properly sanitize or encode user input before rendering it in HTML contexts[3]. Developers should implement input validation, output encoding, and Content Security Policy (CSP) headers to prevent such attacks[2].\",\"engine\":\"Perplexity\"}"[39m at C:\Users\adria\.gemini\antigravity\scratch\kelionai-v2\tests\e2e-full.spec.js:1810:24
